title: FamilyRelationships
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes. We then define:

  1. The set of parents of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
  2. The set of parents of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
  3. The set of children of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
  4. The set of children of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
  5. The set of siblings of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
  6. The set of ancestors of $v$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
  7. The set of ancestors of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
  8. The set of descendants of $v$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
  9. The set of descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
  10. The set of non-descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
  11. The strongly connected component of $v$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
  12. The (union of) strongly connected components of $A$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
  13. The district of $v$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
  14. The district of $A$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_5 -- "FamilyRelationships".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_5
% title: FamilyRelationships
\def\rowref{def\_3\_5}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_5}

\begin{Def}[Family relationships]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and the walk definitions of def \ref{def:walks}. Let $v, w \in J \cup V$ and let $A \ins J \cup V$ be a subset of nodes. We adopt the convention that each individual form $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Sib^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, $\Dist^G(v)$ defined below is defined for any $v \in J \cup V$ (not only $v \in V$); in particular, input nodes $v \in J$ admit the same set-builder definitions, and each set form $\Pa^G(A)$, $\Ch^G(A)$, $\Anc^G(A)$, $\Desc^G(A)$, $\Sc^G(A)$, $\Dist^G(A)$ is well-typed for $A \ins J \cup V$ because every summand $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, $\Dist^G(v)$ is defined.

    \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
        \item \emph{Parents.}
            The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Pa^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (w, v) \in E \rC.
            \]
            The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).
            \]

        \item \emph{Children.}
            The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Ch^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (v, w) \in E \rC.
            \]
            The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Ch^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).
            \]

        \item \emph{Siblings.}
            The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Sib^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (v, w) \in L \rC.
            \]

        \item \emph{Ancestors.}
            The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Anc^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a directed walk from } w \text{ to } v \text{ in } G \rC,
            \]
            where ``directed walk'' is in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. In particular, the length-$0$ (trivial) directed walk at $v$ (admitted by def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., as the single-vertex tuple $(v)$ with no edges) witnesses $v \in \Anc^G(v)$. Hence
            \[
                v \in \Anc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V,
            \]
            \emph{unconditionally}: this holds regardless of whether any edge (in particular any directed self-loop) incident to $v$ exists in $G$.

            The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Anc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v),
            \]
            and consequently $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

        \item \emph{Descendants.}
            The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Desc^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a directed walk from } v \text{ to } w \text{ in } G \rC,
            \]
            with ``directed walk'' as in def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. The length-$0$ (trivial) directed walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Desc^G(v)$, so
            \[
                v \in \Desc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V,
            \]
            \emph{unconditionally} (regardless of whether any edge, in particular any directed self-loop, incident to $v$ exists in $G$).

            The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Desc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v),
            \]
            and consequently $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

        \item \emph{Non-descendants.}
            The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).
            \]

        \item \emph{Strongly connected component.}
            The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).
            \]
            Since $v \in \Anc^G(v)$ and $v \in \Desc^G(v)$ unconditionally (items~iv.\ and~v.), it follows that
            \[
                v \in \Sc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V,
            \]
            \emph{unconditionally} (regardless of whether any edge incident to $v$ exists in $G$).

            The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v),
            \]
            and consequently $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

        \item \emph{District.}
            The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a bidirected walk from } v \text{ to } w \text{ in } G \rC,
            \]
            where ``bidirected walk'' is in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~iii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. The lecture notes' displayed shape ``$v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w$'' is to be read here purely as syntactic sugar for ``walk all of whose edges lie in $L$''; the intermediate-node indexing $v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}$ imposes no lower bound on $n$, and in particular admits $n = 0$. The length-$0$ (trivial) bidirected walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Dist^G(v)$, so
            \[
                v \in \Dist^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V,
            \]
            \emph{unconditionally}: neither a bidirected self-loop at $v$ nor a length-$2$ bidirected cycle through $v$ is required for self-membership (and indeed a bidirected self-loop at $v$ is excluded a priori by the irreflexivity of $L$ in def \ref{def-cdmg}).

            The \emph{district} of $A$ in $G$ is
            \[
                \Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v),
            \]
            and consequently $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$ unconditionally.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the walk definitions of def def:walks. Let $v, w \in J \cup V$ and let $A \ins J \cup V$ be a subset of nodes. We adopt the convention that each individual form $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Sib^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, $\Dist^G(v)$ defined below is defined for any $v \in J \cup V$ (not only $v \in V$); in particular, input nodes $v \in J$ admit the same set-builder definitions, and each set form $\Pa^G(A)$, $\Ch^G(A)$, $\Anc^G(A)$, $\Desc^G(A)$, $\Sc^G(A)$, $\Dist^G(A)$ is well-typed for $A \ins J \cup V$ because every summand $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, $\Dist^G(v)$ is defined.

  1. Parents. The set of parents of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Pa^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (w, v) \in E \rC. \] The set of parents of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v). \]
  2. Children. The set of children of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Ch^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (v, w) \in E \rC. \] The set of children of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Ch^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v). \]
  3. Siblings. The set of siblings of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Sib^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, (v, w) \in L \rC. \]
  4. Ancestors. The set of ancestors of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Anc^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a directed walk from } w \text{ to } v \text{ in } G \rC, \] where ŌĆ£directed walkŌĆØ is in the sense of def def:walks, item ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. In particular, the length-$0$ (trivial) directed walk at $v$ (admitted by def def:walks, item ii., as the single-vertex tuple $(v)$ with no edges) witnesses $v \in \Anc^G(v)$. Hence \[ v \in \Anc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \] unconditionally: this holds regardless of whether any edge (in particular any directed self-loop) incident to $v$ exists in $G$.

    The set of ancestors of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Anc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v), \] and consequently $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

  5. Descendants. The set of descendants of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Desc^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a directed walk from } v \text{ to } w \text{ in } G \rC, \] with ŌĆ£directed walkŌĆØ as in def def:walks, item ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. The length-$0$ (trivial) directed walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Desc^G(v)$, so \[ v \in \Desc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \] unconditionally (regardless of whether any edge, in particular any directed self-loop, incident to $v$ exists in $G$).

    The set of descendants of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Desc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v), \] and consequently $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

  6. Non-descendants. The set of non-descendants of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A). \]
  7. Strongly connected component. The strongly connected component of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v). \] Since $v \in \Anc^G(v)$ and $v \in \Desc^G(v)$ unconditionally (items iv.\ and v.), it follows that \[ v \in \Sc^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \] unconditionally (regardless of whether any edge incident to $v$ exists in $G$).

    The (union of) strongly connected components of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v), \] and consequently $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$ unconditionally.

  8. District. The district of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, \text{there exists a bidirected walk from } v \text{ to } w \text{ in } G \rC, \] where ŌĆ£bidirected walkŌĆØ is in the sense of def def:walks, item iii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$. The lecture notes' displayed shape ŌĆ£$v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w$ŌĆØ is to be read here purely as syntactic sugar for ŌĆ£walk all of whose edges lie in $L$ŌĆØ; the intermediate-node indexing $v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}$ imposes no lower bound on $n$, and in particular admits $n = 0$. The length-$0$ (trivial) bidirected walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Dist^G(v)$, so \[ v \in \Dist^G(v) \qquad \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \] unconditionally: neither a bidirected self-loop at $v$ nor a length-$2$ bidirected cycle through $v$ is required for self-membership (and indeed a bidirected self-loop at $v$ is excluded a priori by the irreflexivity of $L$ in def def-cdmg).

    The district of $A$ in $G$ is \[ \Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v), \] and consequently $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$ unconditionally.

lean_blocks.code:
def Pa (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ (w, v) Ōłł G.E}

def PaSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Pa v

def Ch (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ (v, w) Ōłł G.E}

def ChSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Ch v

def Sib (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ (v, w) Ōłł G.L}

def Anc (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā p : Walk G w v, p.IsDirectedWalk}

def AncSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Anc v

def Desc (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā p : Walk G v w, p.IsDirectedWalk}

def DescSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Desc v

def NonDesc (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  ((G.J Ōł¬ G.V : Finset Node) : Set Node) \ G.DescSet A

def Sc (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  G.Anc v Ōł® G.Desc v

def ScSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Sc v

def Dist (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  {w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ Ōłā p : Walk G v w, p.IsBidirectedWalk}

def DistSet (G : CDMG Node) (A : Set Node) : Set Node :=
  Ōŗā v Ōłł A, G.Dist v

addition_to_the_LN:
[self_membership_notes_require_length_zero_walks] In the definitions of $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$, the directed and bidirected walks are understood to include the trivial walk of length $0$ consisting of the single vertex $v$ with no edges. Consequently, $v \in \Anc^G(v)$, $v \in \Desc^G(v)$, $v \in \Sc^G(v)$, and $v \in \Dist^G(v)$ hold for every $v \in V$ unconditionally, regardless of whether any edge (in particular any self-loop) incident to $v$ exists in $G$.

[type_mismatch_individual_vs_set_versions] The preamble should read "$v, w \in J \cup V$" rather than "$v, w \in V$": the individual definitions $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Sib^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$ are defined for any $v \in J \cup V$, using the same set-builder expressions given. In particular, input nodes ($v \in J$) may have children, descendants, etc., and the set-version definitions $\Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)$ (and analogously for the other operators) are well-typed for $A \ins J \cup V$ because each summand is defined.

[district_walk_indexing_ambiguous_for_small_n] $w \in \Dist^G(v)$ iff there exists a walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ in which every edge is bidirected ($\huh$). The walk may have any length $\geq 0$; in particular, the length-$0$ walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Dist^G(v)$, so neither a bidirected self-loop at $v$ nor a length-$2$ bidirected cycle through $v$ is required. The intermediate-node indexing $v_1, \ldots, v_{n-1}$ in the displayed formula should be read as syntactic sugar for "walk consisting of bidirected edges" and imposes no lower bound on walk length.
